verses.text: Elisha had warned the Shunammite of a seven-year famine, so she sojourned in Philistia; now she returns to reclaim her house and land. The prophet's foresight provided for her. God guides His own through coming troubles, often by a word given long before the need.

Elisha had warned the Shunammite of a seven-year famine, so she sojourned in Philistia; now she returns to reclaim her house and land. The prophet's foresight provided for her. God guides His own through coming troubles, often by a word given long before the need.

Elisha had warned the Shunammite of a seven-year famine, so she sojourned in Philistia; now she returns to reclaim her house and land. The prophet's foresight provided for her. God guides His own through coming troubles, often by a word given long before the need.

As the king hears Gehazi tell of the boy Elisha raised, the very woman arrives to appeal, and the king restores all her land and its produce. The timing is no accident. God arranges the moment so the faithful find favour exactly when they need it.

As the king hears Gehazi tell of the boy Elisha raised, the very woman arrives to appeal, and the king restores all her land and its produce. The timing is no accident. God arranges the moment so the faithful find favour exactly when they need it.

As the king hears Gehazi tell of the boy Elisha raised, the very woman arrives to appeal, and the king restores all her land and its produce. The timing is no accident. God arranges the moment so the faithful find favour exactly when they need it.

Elisha comes to Damascus, where the sick Ben-hadad sends Hazael to ask if he will recover; Elisha says he may recover, yet will surely die. The prophet sees both the illness and the murder to come. God's servants discern what lies beneath a polite inquiry.

Elisha comes to Damascus, where the sick Ben-hadad sends Hazael to ask if he will recover; Elisha says he may recover, yet will surely die. The prophet sees both the illness and the murder to come. God's servants discern what lies beneath a polite inquiry.

Elisha comes to Damascus, where the sick Ben-hadad sends Hazael to ask if he will recover; Elisha says he may recover, yet will surely die. The prophet sees both the illness and the murder to come. God's servants discern what lies beneath a polite inquiry.

Elisha comes to Damascus, where the sick Ben-hadad sends Hazael to ask if he will recover; Elisha says he may recover, yet will surely die. The prophet sees both the illness and the murder to come. God's servants discern what lies beneath a polite inquiry.

Elisha stares at Hazael till he is ashamed, then weeps, foreseeing the cruelty Hazael will inflict on Israel. Hazael protests he is but a dog. The man of God grieves over evils others cannot yet imagine they will do; sin's future is darker than we think.

Elisha stares at Hazael till he is ashamed, then weeps, foreseeing the cruelty Hazael will inflict on Israel. Hazael protests he is but a dog. The man of God grieves over evils others cannot yet imagine they will do; sin's future is darker than we think.

Elisha stares at Hazael till he is ashamed, then weeps, foreseeing the cruelty Hazael will inflict on Israel. Hazael protests he is but a dog. The man of God grieves over evils others cannot yet imagine they will do; sin's future is darker than we think.

Hazael returns, lies to his master that he will live, then smothers him and seizes the throne. The murder Elisha foresaw is done. A heart that says 'am I a dog?' today can commit, tomorrow, the very atrocity it once thought unthinkable.

Hazael returns, lies to his master that he will live, then smothers him and seizes the throne. The murder Elisha foresaw is done. A heart that says 'am I a dog?' today can commit, tomorrow, the very atrocity it once thought unthinkable.

In Judah, Jehoram reigns, walks in Ahab's ways through marriage to Ahab's daughter, and does evil; yet for David's sake God will not destroy Judah, keeping him a lamp. The covenant with David holds back the judgment an evil king deserves. Mercy spares for a promise's sake.

In Judah, Jehoram reigns, walks in Ahab's ways through marriage to Ahab's daughter, and does evil; yet for David's sake God will not destroy Judah, keeping him a lamp. The covenant with David holds back the judgment an evil king deserves. Mercy spares for a promise's sake.

In his days Edom revolts and breaks free, and Libnah too. The kingdom's grip loosens under an unfaithful king. When rulers forsake God, the territory and security their fathers held begin to slip from their hands.

In his days Edom revolts and breaks free, and Libnah too. The kingdom's grip loosens under an unfaithful king. When rulers forsake God, the territory and security their fathers held begin to slip from their hands.

In his days Edom revolts and breaks free, and Libnah too. The kingdom's grip loosens under an unfaithful king. When rulers forsake God, the territory and security their fathers held begin to slip from their hands.
Jehoram dies and is buried, and his son Ahaziah reigns. The brief, evil reign passes. Kings come and go in quick succession, but the throne's true keeper is the God who set the line of David upon it.
Jehoram dies and is buried, and his son Ahaziah reigns. The brief, evil reign passes. Kings come and go in quick succession, but the throne's true keeper is the God who set the line of David upon it.

Ahaziah of Judah, grandson of Ahab through his mother Athaliah, walks in Ahab's wicked ways, for that house counsels him. A bad marriage-alliance bears poisoned fruit a generation on. The influence of an ungodly family reaches down through the children's children.

Ahaziah of Judah, grandson of Ahab through his mother Athaliah, walks in Ahab's wicked ways, for that house counsels him. A bad marriage-alliance bears poisoned fruit a generation on. The influence of an ungodly family reaches down through the children's children.

Ahaziah of Judah, grandson of Ahab through his mother Athaliah, walks in Ahab's wicked ways, for that house counsels him. A bad marriage-alliance bears poisoned fruit a generation on. The influence of an ungodly family reaches down through the children's children.

Ahaziah joins Joram of Israel against Hazael, and Joram is wounded and goes to Jezreel to recover, with Ahaziah visiting him. The two evil houses are drawn together at Jezreel — the very place where God's judgment, through Jehu, is about to fall on them both.
